name: Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) Identity Design
description: Designing identity solutions using Microsoft Entra ID including hybrid identity, external identities, and Managed Identities for Azure resources.
key_facts: Microsoft Entra ID is the cloud identity provider; on-premises AD DS connects via Entra ID Connect for hybrid identity
Three sync methods: Password Hash Sync (PHS), Pass-Through Authentication (PTA), and Federation (ADFS)
Managed Identities (System-assigned and User-assigned) eliminate the need to store credentials in application code
External Identities enable B2B collaboration with partner organizations and B2C for consumer-facing apps
Conditional Access policies enforce Zero Trust by requiring MFA, compliant devices, or specific locations before granting access
common_misconceptions: Architects assume PTA provides the same resilience as PHS—PTA requires on-premises agents and can fail if agents are down; PHS syncs hashes to the cloud for cloud-only authentication
Many believe Managed Identities can be used for all Azure services—not all Azure services support Managed Identity as a principal in their access control

name: Conditional Access and Zero Trust Architecture
description: Designing Conditional Access policies to enforce Zero Trust security including MFA requirements, device compliance, and risk-based access.
key_facts: Conditional Access policies have Assignments (who/what/where) and Access Controls (grant/session controls)
Named locations allow IP ranges and countries to be defined for location-based conditions
Sign-in risk and user risk policies integrate with Entra ID Protection for risk-based Conditional Access
Report-only mode tests the impact of policies without enforcing them, essential before going live
Session controls enable app-enforced restrictions, continuous access evaluation, and sign-in frequency
common_misconceptions: Architects assume Conditional Access requires Azure AD Premium P2—Conditional Access requires P1; P2 adds risk-based policies and Identity Governance
Many believe a 'Block all' policy is the most secure starting point—this can lock out administrators; always use break-glass emergency access accounts

name: Azure RBAC, Management Groups, and Governance
description: Designing governance hierarchies using Management Groups, Subscriptions, Azure Policy, Blueprints, and RBAC for large enterprise environments.
key_facts: Management Groups provide a hierarchy above subscriptions; policies and RBAC at this level inherit down to all subscriptions
Azure Policy can audit, deny, or automatically remediate non-compliant resources using built-in and custom definitions
RBAC roles are additive; the most permissive role at any scope applies (deny assignments are an exception)
Azure Blueprints package policies, RBAC, ARM templates, and resource groups for repeatable, governed environment creation
Landing Zones use a management group hierarchy with defined policy sets for enterprise-scale governance
common_misconceptions: Architects assume Azure Policy stops deployments in real time—Policy effects are: Deny (blocking), Audit (logging only), Append, Modify, DeployIfNotExists (remediation); not all are blocking
Many believe Blueprints have replaced ARM templates—Blueprints orchestrate multiple artifacts including ARM templates; they are complementary

name: Azure Monitor and Observability Design
description: Designing comprehensive monitoring solutions using Azure Monitor, Log Analytics, Application Insights, and Azure Sentinel.
key_facts: Azure Monitor is the unified monitoring platform collecting metrics (numeric, time-series) and logs (structured records)
Log Analytics workspaces store and query log data using Kusto Query Language (KQL)
Application Insights provides APM including distributed tracing, live metrics, and failure analysis for apps
Diagnostic Settings send platform logs and metrics to Log Analytics, Event Hubs, or Storage Account
Azure Monitor Alerts trigger actions via Action Groups (email, SMS, webhook, Logic App, Azure Function) based on metric or log conditions
common_misconceptions: Architects assume all Azure resources send logs to Azure Monitor automatically—Diagnostic Settings must be explicitly configured per resource
Many believe Log Analytics and Application Insights are separate services—Application Insights is now workspace-based and stores data in a Log Analytics workspace

name: Azure Storage Account Design and Tiering
description: Designing Azure Storage solutions with appropriate account types, redundancy options, access tiers, and lifecycle management for cost and performance requirements.
key_facts: Redundancy options: LRS (3 copies, 1 DC), ZRS (3 zones, 1 region), GRS (6 copies, 2 regions), GZRS (zones + geo)
Blob access tiers: Hot (frequent access), Cool (infrequent, 30-day minimum), Cold (rare, 90-day minimum), Archive (offline, 180-day minimum)
Premium storage accounts use SSDs for low-latency; available for Block Blobs, File Shares, and Page Blobs
Immutable Blob storage (WORM) supports time-based retention and legal hold policies for compliance
Azure Files supports SMB and NFS protocols; integrates with on-premises via Azure File Sync for hybrid scenarios
common_misconceptions: Architects assume Archive tier data can be read immediately—Archive-tier blobs must be rehydrated to Hot or Cool tier (can take hours) before they can be read
Many believe GRS guarantees failover will restore all data—GRS offers an RPO of ~1 hour; some recent writes may be lost during failover

name: Azure Database Service Selection and Design
description: Selecting and designing appropriate Azure database services (SQL Database, Cosmos DB, PostgreSQL, MySQL) based on workload requirements.
key_facts: Azure SQL Database Hyperscale scales to 100 TB and separates compute from storage for independent scaling
Cosmos DB supports multiple APIs: NoSQL, MongoDB, Cassandra, Gremlin (graph), and Table
Cosmos DB consistency levels (strongest to weakest): Strong, Bounded Staleness, Session, Consistent Prefix, Eventual
Azure SQL Managed Instance provides near 100% SQL Server compatibility for lift-and-shift migrations
Elastic pools enable resource sharing among multiple SQL Databases for bursty workloads at lower cost
common_misconceptions: Architects assume Cosmos DB automatic indexing has no cost—all properties are indexed by default; excluding unused properties from the index reduces RU costs
Many believe Azure SQL MI and Azure SQL Database are equivalent—Managed Instance supports SQL Agent, CLR, linked servers, and Service Broker which Database does not

name: High Availability and Disaster Recovery Design
description: Designing HA/DR solutions using Availability Zones, Azure Site Recovery, geo-redundant services, and traffic management for RPO/RTO requirements.
key_facts: Availability Zones (AZs) are physically separate data centers within a region with independent power, cooling, and networking
Azure Site Recovery replicates VMs, physical servers, and VMware/Hyper-V workloads for DR with RPO ~30 seconds
Azure SQL auto-failover groups provide transparent failover for SQL databases with a read-write listener endpoint
Traffic Manager uses DNS-based routing with health probes; failover depends on DNS TTL propagation (not instant)
Azure Front Door provides global HTTP/S load balancing with instant failover using anycast and active health probes
common_misconceptions: Architects assume Traffic Manager provides instant failover—Traffic Manager uses DNS with TTL; failover can take minutes depending on client DNS caching

Many believe deploying to two AZs doubles cost—zone-redundant services like Azure SQL have no additional cost for AZ redundancy; only the extra compute/storage for active-active configurations adds cost

name: Azure Compute Service Selection and Design
description: Selecting appropriate Azure compute services (VMs, AKS, App Service, Container Instances, Azure Functions) based on workload requirements.
key_facts: Azure App Service Plan tiers: Shared (F/D), Basic (B), Standard (S), Premium (P), Isolated (I for ASE); only Standard+ supports autoscale
AKS (Azure Kubernetes Service) manages the Kubernetes control plane for free; you pay only for agent node VMs
Azure Container Instances (ACI) is serverless; suitable for burst workloads and batch jobs without cluster management
Spot VMs offer up to 90% discount but can be evicted with 30-second notice when Azure needs capacity back
Virtual Machine Scale Sets (VMSS) provide autoscaling of identical VMs; Flexible orchestration mode supports mixing VM sizes
common_misconceptions: Architects assume Azure Functions can run indefinitely—Consumption plan functions timeout at 10 minutes; Premium and Dedicated plans can be configured for unlimited timeout
Many believe AKS is always more expensive than App Service for containerized workloads—for high-density multi-tenant scenarios AKS can be more cost-effective

name: Azure Virtual Network and Hub-Spoke Design
description: Designing VNet architectures including hub-and-spoke topology, peering, ExpressRoute, VPN Gateway, and network security groups.
key_facts: Hub-spoke topology centralizes shared services (firewall, DNS, monitoring) in hub VNet with spoke VNets peered to it
VNet peering is non-transitive; traffic from spoke A cannot reach spoke B through hub without additional routing or Azure Firewall
Azure Virtual WAN automates hub-and-spoke at scale with managed routing and branch connectivity
ExpressRoute connects on-premises to Azure via private peering through a connectivity provider; bypasses the public internet
Azure Firewall Premium adds IDPS (Intrusion Detection and Prevention), TLS inspection, and URL filtering over Standard
common_misconceptions: Architects assume VNet peering is transitive by default—peering is non-transitive; routing between spokes requires user-defined routes through the hub or Azure Firewall
Many believe ExpressRoute failover to VPN Gateway is automatic—explicit failover configuration using BGP route preferences and AS path manipulation is required

name: Azure Migration Strategy and Azure Migrate
description: Designing migration strategies using Azure Migrate for discovery, assessment, and migration of on-premises workloads to Azure.
key_facts: Azure Migrate is the hub for discovery, assessment, and migration of VMs, databases, and web apps
Migration strategies (5Rs): Rehost (lift-and-shift), Refactor (PaaS migration), Rearchitect, Rebuild, Replace
Server Assessment creates business cases comparing on-premises TCO versus Azure cost with sizing recommendations
Azure Migrate supports agentless VMware migration and agent-based migration for Hyper-V and physical servers
Database Migration Service (DMS) migrates SQL Server, MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, and MongoDB databases to Azure
common_misconceptions: Architects assume rehost (lift-and-shift) is always the fastest migration—it is the fastest initially but leaves cost optimization opportunities unrealized compared to refactoring to PaaS
Many believe Azure Migrate can migrate all workloads without any downtime—most migration methods have a cutover window; only a few support zero-downtime continuous replication

name: Microservices and Event-Driven Architecture
description: Designing microservices and event-driven solutions using Azure Service Bus, Event Grid, API Management, and Azure Service Fabric.
key_facts: Azure Service Bus supports queues (competing consumers) and topics/subscriptions (publish-subscribe) for reliable messaging
Event Grid is a serverless event routing service for reactive programming with 24-hour retry and dead-letter support
API Management provides a facade for backend APIs with rate limiting, caching, transformation, and subscription management
Azure Service Bus premium tier supports message sessions for FIFO ordering and large messages up to 100 MB
The Competing Consumer pattern scales message processing by having multiple workers read from the same queue
common_misconceptions: Architects confuse Event Grid and Event Hubs—Event Grid routes discrete events (resource state changes) while Event Hubs streams high-volume telemetry data
Many assume API Management must be internet-facing—APIM can be deployed in internal VNet mode accessible only from private networks

name: Azure Cost Optimization Design Principles
description: Designing cost-efficient Azure solutions using Reserved Instances, Azure Hybrid Benefit, autoscaling, and Azure Cost Management.
key_facts: Reserved Instances (1 or 3 year) save up to 72% versus pay-as-you-go for stable, predictable workloads
Azure Hybrid Benefit applies existing Windows Server and SQL Server licenses to Azure VMs and PaaS services
Autoscaling reduces costs by right-sizing compute to actual demand instead of provisioning for peak capacity
Azure Advisor analyzes usage and provides cost, security, reliability, and performance recommendations
Dev/Test pricing provides discounted rates for non-production workloads in EA and MCA subscriptions
common_misconceptions: Architects assume Reserved Instances are tied to a specific VM—Reservations are flexible by instance size flexibility within the same VM family and region
Many believe Spot VMs are suitable for all workloads—Spot VMs are only appropriate for fault-tolerant, interruption-tolerant workloads like batch processing

name: Azure Backup and Recovery Design
description: Designing backup solutions using Azure Backup, Recovery Services Vaults, and backup policies for VMs, SQL, and files.
key_facts: Recovery Services Vault stores backup data for Azure VMs, SQL in VM, Azure Files, and on-premises workloads
Azure Backup Center provides a unified management view across multiple vaults and subscriptions
Soft delete retains deleted backup data for 14 days to protect against accidental or malicious deletion
Cross-Region Restore (CRR) allows restoring VMs to a paired region from geo-redundant vaults
Azure Backup Reports uses Log Analytics to provide insights on backup jobs, storage consumption, and compliance
common_misconceptions: Architects assume backup and DR serve the same purpose—backup protects against data loss/corruption; DR protects against full site/region failure; both are needed
Many believe Azure Backup automatically protects new VMs—backup policies must be explicitly assigned to VMs; new VMs are not automatically protected

name: Azure Well-Architected Framework
description: Applying the Azure Well-Architected Framework's five pillars to evaluate and improve architectural decisions.
key_facts: The five WAF pillars are: Reliability, Security, Cost Optimization, Operational Excellence, and Performance Efficiency
Azure Well-Architected Review is an assessment tool that scores workloads against WAF principles
Reliability pillar focuses on designing for failure, resilience, and meeting defined RTO/RPO targets
Performance Efficiency pillar emphasizes scaling, caching, and choosing the right service for the workload
Operational Excellence pillar covers deployment automation, monitoring, and continuous improvement processes
common_misconceptions: Teams assume WAF reviews are one-time activities—WAF recommends periodic reviews as architectures and usage patterns evolve
Many believe WAF is Azure-specific guidance with no cross-cloud relevance—the principles are universal; only specific service recommendations are Azure-specific

name: Azure Private Link and Private Endpoints
description: Designing private connectivity for Azure PaaS services using Private Endpoints and Private Link Service to eliminate public exposure.
key_facts: Private Endpoint assigns a private IP from your VNet to a specific Azure PaaS resource instance
Private DNS Zones must be linked to VNets for name resolution to work; one zone per service type (e.g., privatelink.blob.core.windows.net)
Private Link Service allows exposing your own services behind a Standard Load Balancer for private consumption
Service Endpoints route traffic over the Microsoft backbone but the PaaS service still has a public IP; Private Endpoints are more secure
Network policies for private endpoints must be disabled on the subnet before creating a Private Endpoint
common_misconceptions: Architects treat Service Endpoints and Private Endpoints as equivalent security controls—Private Endpoints provide a private IP and fully remove public exposure; Service Endpoints only restrict which VNets can access the public endpoint
Many assume Private Endpoints work automatically across peered VNets—Private DNS Zone VNet links must include all VNets that need private resolution, including peered VNets
